text.title: Consistency Implementation Patterns
text.summary: Implementation patterns for visual, functional, and verbal consistency, including design system requirements.

rule.statement: Define a design token system before writing component code; retrofitting tokens into existing code costs 5x more than starting with them.
rule.rationale: Without tokens, every developer hardcodes colors, spacing, and typography values, producing a combinatorial explosion of inconsistencies that no audit can fully correct.

example.note: Color tokens: primary-action always #0066CC, destructive always #CC0000, success always #00CC66. Spacing: base 8px, margins are multiples (8, 16, 24, 32).

rule.statement: Establish behavior standards for navigation and interaction that hold across every screen, with no exceptions without documentation.
rule.rationale: One inconsistent screen breaks the learned behavior for the whole product. Users encounter the exception, fail, then begin to distrust every interaction.

example.note: Logo click: always goes to home. Back button: always goes to previous page. Search: always in top area. Single click: select. Double click: open/edit.
Page 1: [Cancel] [Save]. Page 2: [Save] [Cancel]. Button order reversed — user trained by page 1 will accidentally click Save when trying to Cancel.

rule.statement: Document every component with: visual examples, usage guidelines, do/don't rules, and accessibility notes — not just appearance specs.
rule.rationale: A component spec that shows only appearance is incomplete. Without usage guidelines, developers apply components in wrong contexts, producing functional inconsistencies despite visual uniformity.

list.item: Typography: font families, sizes, weights (heading hierarchy and body)
Colors: full palette with usage rules per semantic role
Spacing: base unit and grid rules
Buttons: all variants (primary, secondary, destructive, ghost) and all states
Form elements: inputs, selects, checkboxes, radio buttons
Icons: consistent icon set with label requirements

rule.statement: Run a consistency audit at the start of each major feature cycle, not at the end; fixing mid-feature is cheaper than post-release.
rule.rationale: Design drift accumulates continuously. Post-release consistency fixes require user communication, staged rollouts, and regression testing. Mid-cycle fixes are local and contained.
